nodes.label: Sean
nodes.title: Project Lead
nodes.scope: End-to-end project management for the NOP Risk Model modernization. Sean coordinates between the technical team, Dr. Aris (external consultant), regional inspectors, and executive leadership. Responsible for SOW procurement, stakeholder alignment, milestone tracking, and escalation to Marc.
nodes.bio: Sean leads the NOP project team and serves as the primary interface between technical contributors and executive decision-makers. He managed the transition from Scenario A to Scenario B and coordinated the IAD audit process.

nodes.label: Stephen
nodes.title: Internal Auditor, IAD
nodes.scope: Independent internal auditor from the Internal Audit Directorate (IAD). Stephen operates outside the project team and is responsible for evaluating the NOP Risk Model against the Four Pillars framework: data integrity, algorithmic transparency, human oversight, and regulatory compliance. He delivered the final 'Fully Compliant' audit rating in March 2027.
nodes.bio: Stephen's audit covered the full project lifecycle — from data sourcing through production deployment. His independence from the project team is structurally important: he reports to a separate directorate, not to Marc.

nodes.label: Dr. Aris
nodes.title: External Statistical Consultant
nodes.scope: Engaged under the project SOW to design the updated risk model algorithm and provide statistical methodology expertise. Dr. Aris proposed the original Scenario A (10% geographic weight) and the initial algorithm structure. His engagement was scoped to the development and review phases — he was not involved in production deployment or the IAD audit.
nodes.bio: Dr. Aris brought external statistical rigour to the model design. Although his Scenario A was ultimately revised to Scenario B, his algorithmic framework formed the foundation of the final model.

nodes.label: Jason Hao
nodes.title: Technical Lead & Senior SAS Developer
nodes.scope: Primary technical contributor for the NOP Risk Model. Jason is responsible for all SAS development, data analysis, and production model deployment. He identified the 1900-01-01 legacy data decay artifact (11,240 records), conducted the Scenario B sensitivity analysis that drove the 15% geographic weight decision, wrote all production SAS code, and led the UAT parallel run. Primary contact for any technical questions about the model algorithm, SAS implementation, or data pipeline.
nodes.bio: Jason is the most experienced SAS developer on the team and the de facto technical authority on the risk scoring algorithm. His sensitivity analysis overturned Dr. Aris's initial Scenario A recommendation.

nodes.label: Roger
nodes.title: Senior Analyst, QA
nodes.scope: Quality assurance for all SAS code and model outputs. Roger runs UAT parallel validation, verifies detection rate metrics, and signs off on performance benchmarks before production deployment. He reviews every SAS macro written by Jason and maintains the QA log. Contact for model validation results, UAT reports, and performance metrics.
nodes.bio: Roger is the QA checkpoint between technical development and production deployment. His parallel run validation confirmed the new model's 18% improvement in detection rates.

nodes.label: Dave
nodes.title: Regional Inspector, Pacific Region
nodes.scope: Represents the Pacific regional perspective on inspection risk. Dave provides operational ground-truth on dangerous goods inspection priorities, with specific expertise in the Vancouver transload corridor and ERAP (Emergency Response Assistance Plan) site handling. He was the primary advocate for raising the geographic weight and raised the concern that ERAP sites must never be streamlined regardless of score.
nodes.bio: Dave's regional experience shaped two key model decisions: the 15% geographic weight and the ERAP override rule. His pushback on Scenario A was instrumental in the team reconsidering the methodology.

nodes.label: Marc
nodes.title: Director, Transportation Safety Programs
nodes.scope: Executive sponsor and final decision authority for the NOP Risk Model project. Marc holds budget approval authority and signed off on both the 15% geographic weight (Scenario B) and the STRL-2026 streamlining filter. Responsible for ensuring the project meets Treasury Board Secretariat directives on automated decision-making.
nodes.bio: Marc approved the final model configuration after reviewing Jason's sensitivity analysis and the team's scenario comparison. His mandate was zero budget growth — the streamlining filter was introduced specifically to satisfy this constraint.

nodes.label: Carolyn
nodes.title: Data Management Manager
nodes.scope: Owns the TDG_CORE database and data dictionary. Carolyn leads data governance for the project — she is the primary authority on field definitions, schema changes, and data quality standards. She led the remediation of the 1900-01-01 legacy date artifact and manages the Data & Technical Sub-team. Contact for any questions about the TDG_CORE schema, data dictionary, or data governance policy.
nodes.bio: Carolyn manages the underlying data infrastructure that feeds the risk model. Her team's discovery and cleanup of the legacy decay issue was a prerequisite for the model rebuild.

nodes.label: NOP Risk Model Project Team
nodes.goal: Modernize the dangerous goods site inspection risk scoring model to improve detection rates, reduce false negatives, and maintain budget neutrality through the STRL-2026 streamlining filter.
nodes.description: Cross-functional team responsible for the 2026-27 modernization of the National Occupational Profile (NOP) risk model used to prioritize dangerous goods site inspections across Canada. The team rebuilt the model from a failing 2015 baseline (14% false negative rate) to a fully compliant, audited production system.

nodes.label: Data & Technical Sub-team
nodes.goal: Own TDG_CORE database integrity, SAS model implementation, and all data governance for the risk model.
nodes.description: Responsible for data quality, model coding, and technical validation. This sub-team identified and remediated the 1900-01-01 legacy data decay issue affecting 11,240 records, produced the production SAS model, and ran UAT parallel validation before go-live.
